params.text:
Information sharing done right

Good safeguarding depends on the right people sharing the right information at the right time. Practitioners sometimes hold back because they worry about data protection law – but fear of breaching confidentiality or GDPR should never be a reason to leave a child at risk.

  • The Data Protection Act 2018 and UK GDPR allow you to share information to safeguard a child. Data protection is not a barrier to sharing where a child may be at risk.
  • Share what is necessary and proportionate, with those who need it, and record what you shared and why.
  • You should normally be open and honest with the family about sharing information – unless doing so would put the child (or someone else) at greater risk.
  • If in doubt, seek advice from your DSL, but never delay sharing where a child is in immediate danger.

params.question: Which statement about sharing information to safeguard a child is correct?

answers.text: Data protection law allows information to be shared when a child may be at risk

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Data protection and GDPR are not barriers to safeguarding. Necessary, proportionate sharing to protect a child is permitted.

answers.text: GDPR means you can never share a child's information without their parents' consent

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Consent is not always required. Where a child may be at risk, information can be shared without consent, and sometimes it must be.

answers.text: You should always tell the alleged abuser what has been shared

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: You should not tip off an alleged abuser. Being open with the family does not extend to compromising a child's safety.

answers.text: It is safer to share nothing at all than to risk a data breach

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Holding information back can leave a child in danger. Fear of a data breach must never override a child's safety.
